mnemonics: PUP Threads: Pthreads (POSIX), User-level, and Pthreads again for good measure! Remember: 'Pick Up the Pace' with thread libraries.
keyPoints: Thread libraries provide an API for creating and managing threads, hiding the implementation details from the programmer.

The three main types are: 1) POSIX Pthreads (portable across OSes), 2) User-level threads (implemented in a library, e.g., GNU Portable Threads), and 3) Kernel-level threads (supported directly by the OS kernel, e.g., Windows threads).
Hybrid implementations, like Linux's NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library), combine user and kernel-level support for efficiency and flexibility.
commonMistakes: Assuming all thread libraries (like Pthreads) map 1:1 to kernel threads; the mapping is a library implementation detail.
Confusing the thread library API (the interface) with its underlying implementation (user-level, kernel-level, or hybrid).
